humanities_role_explanation: Uses humanities subjects (such as US History, European History, World History, Philosophy, and World Religions) as factual and conceptual multiple-choice QA items to evaluate general multitask language understanding.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only multiple-choice questions with four options (A, B, C, D) presented to models in zero-shot and few-shot learning configurations.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models on their ability to retrieve domain-specific knowledge and perform reasoning, including analyzing historical events, applying legal precedents to complex scenarios, and evaluating moral intuitions.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates general academic, professional, and world knowledge across 57 diverse subjects spanning STEM, humanities, and social sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates GPT-3 (Ada, Babbage, Curie, Davinci / Small, Medium, Large, X-Large), GPT-2, UnifiedQA, RoBERTa, and ALBERT.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Large language models are the primary systems being benchmarked to evaluate their multitask language understanding capabilities.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes multiple humanities subjects such as History (US, European, and World History), Philosophy, World Religions, Jurisprudence, and Business Ethics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates performance using multiple-choice classification accuracy (percentage correct) and Root Mean Squared (RMS) calibration error.

published_after_march_2023.note: The paper was published in ICLR 2021 and preprints were released on arXiv in late 2020.

english_language.note: The paper and all the benchmark questions are written in English.

summary: This paper proposes the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark, a test containing 15,908 multiple-choice questions across 57 academic and professional subjects. It is designed to evaluate the depth and breadth of a model's knowledge and problem-solving abilities in zero-shot and few-shot settings. The benchmark serves as a comprehensive tool to identify models' blind spots across STEM, humanities, and social sciences.
key_findings: The largest GPT-3 model (175B parameters) achieves an average accuracy of 43.9% (significantly outperforming the 25% random baseline), while UnifiedQA (11B) achieves 48.9%. However, performance across subjects is highly lopsided (e.g., GPT-3 scores 69% on US Foreign Policy but near-random on College Chemistry), and the models are poorly calibrated with confidence estimates deviating from actual accuracy by up to 24%.
